pages.full_text:
1 Asteroceras Obtusum

2 [?Dapedium] [?Plesiosaura]

3 Ichthyosaurus

website www.njpublications damon.co.uk

Lyme Bay
pages.notes: Page contains a glued-in postcard/print showing three photographs of Jurassic fossils, captioned 'English Images — Jurassic Fossils'. Handwritten annotations (in pink ink matching the right page) label the fossils: 1 Asteroceras Obtusum (an ammonite), 2 [species name unclear — could be 'Dapedium' (a Jurassic fish) with 'Plesiosauria' or similar], 3 Ichthyosaurus. A website reference 'www.njpublications damon.co.uk' is written in smaller text. 'Lyme Bay' written in pink ink at lower right, slightly rotated. Top-left area has a small box-like marking that may be a page number or layout notation — could read '2' and '3' but unclear if this is a page number or part of the fossil numbering. The second fossil name is very difficult to read; the genus portion may be 'Dapedium' and the species designation resembles 'Plesiosaura' or 'Plesiosauria'. Handwritten page number cannot be confidently read. Some pink text visible along left margin (gutter area) appears cut off.

pages.full_text:
__Monday June 3rd__
	Bleak + cold. We set off for Lyme Regis. It rained + everyone put on rain coats. At 11 we reached the town mill. It was fascinating - a working flour mill with an overshot water mill. [?Restored] a year ago. We 'bought' flour for a donation. They can't sell it because it's different grade flour depending on which volunteer miller is working.
	Then we went to a fascinating [few?] fossil shops. Lovely Lapis Lazuli necklaces. Simon bought John + Charles each little fossils. There was an odd man looking at Tonic + I asked Charles to check he didn't [?let] steal him from where I'd tied him up. He turned out to be a very rude dog warden, a miserable, twisted, old sod, who told me I was committing abandonment, + it allowed him to take Tonic + charge me £60 to get him back etc. + etc... He said Charles had been rude, when all Charles had done was ask other people to watch the warden that he didn't go off with the dog.
	After that it was difficult to find somewhere to leave Tonic + eat. I was very cold. Simon gave me his sweater + we had fish + chips out doors. Then we walked up hill + long roads back to the van. We passed a nature reserve recording the 5 coastal miles of landship 1839. When we reached the van we rested
pages.notes: Written in pink/magenta ink (same as surrounding pages in this volume, consistent with other entries on this trip). Handwritten page number appears to be '68' at top-right, though partially obscured. Date 'Monday June 3rd' is underlined as a heading. Year 2002 inferred from nearby spreads (A_0311 references same trip, same pink ink, same dog 'Tonic'; May 25 2002 was a Saturday, so June 3 2002 was indeed a Monday). The word before 'a year ago' is unclear — possibly 'Restored' or 'Rebuilt'. The phrase 'fascinating [few?]' may read 'fascinating few' or the word before 'fossil shops' is difficult to make out. 'landship 1839' may refer to a landslip/landslide of 1839 (the famous Axmouth-Lyme Regis landslip of December 1839 is well-documented). The entry is cut off at the bottom of the page ('we rested'). 'Tonic' is the family dog (appears in other entries in this volume). 'Simon' appears to be a family member. 'John' and 'Charles' also named.
